At the combined operating point, the hardware performs stable free-running Mackey-Glass prediction and reaches 84.5% blind accuracy on 10-class spoken-digit classification.
main_result: The reservoir demonstrates edge-of-chaos operation with stable chaotic time-series prediction and 84.5% spoken-digit classification accuracy.
application_angle: The design principles support low-energy, reconfigurable optical AI hardware for edge sequential inference.

title: Zero-field dipolar decoupling of color center ensembles via universal qutrit control
abstract: Dipolar interactions are a major source of decoherence in dense ensembles of color centers in diamond. Current protocols demand using bias magnetic fields detrimental in many scenarios. We present ZENITH (Zero-field Ensemble Neutralization via Interleaved Trilevel Handling), a pulsed sequence to cancel dipolar interactions among V-degenerate spin-1 systems. We reveal excellent coherence survival, compatibility with existing sensing sequences, and improved DC detection, advancing a general framework to control interacting degenerate multilevel systems, of broad interest in quantum technologies.
one_liner: ZENITH uses universal qutrit pulses to suppress dipolar decoherence in dense spin-1 color-center ensembles at zero field.
why_it_matters: Zero-field decoupling can improve diamond-based sensing and quantum hardware in settings where bias magnetic fields are disruptive.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces ZENITH, a pulsed control sequence for V-degenerate spin-1 systems that cancels dipolar interactions without applying a bias magnetic field. The protocol targets dense color-center ensembles where dipolar coupling limits coherence. The authors report strong coherence survival, compatibility with established sensing sequences, and improved DC detection performance.
main_result: ZENITH suppresses ensemble dipolar interactions at zero magnetic field while preserving sensing compatibility.
application_angle: The method is directly relevant to compact diamond sensors and multilevel solid-state quantum devices operating without field bias.

title: Genuine Hybrid Number-Polarization Entanglement
abstract: Entanglement is a key resource for fundamental tests of physics and emerging quantum technologies. In quantum optics, two perspectives on entanglement coexist. In the continuous-variable framework, entanglement is understood as holding between optical modes. In contrast, discrete-variable quantum optics focuses on quantum correlations in degrees of freedom such as polarization that label fixed numbers of photons. In this paper, we show that entanglement can transcend this separation. Spontaneous parametric down-conversion inherently generates correlations in optical phase space, photon number, and labelling degrees of freedom simultaneously. In polarization, this structure is traditionally described by macroscopic Bell states. Existing witnesses, however, fail to detect the genuine hybrid entanglement of these states, which goes beyond the continuous-discrete-variable categorization. Here, we lay the groundwork for a general framework unifying continuous- and discrete-variable notions of entanglement. In particular, we derive an operational witness providing a sufficient criterion for genuine hybrid number-polarization entanglement and outline its experimental implementation. Finally, we discuss exemplary states which, together with our results on macroscopic Bell states, motivate a broader classification of genuine hybrid quantum correlations.
one_liner: The paper defines and witnesses genuine hybrid entanglement spanning photon number and polarization degrees of freedom.
why_it_matters: It provides tools to certify optical entanglement beyond the usual continuous-variable versus discrete-variable split.
detailed_summary: The authors argue that spontaneous parametric down-conversion can generate correlations simultaneously in optical phase space, photon number, and labeling degrees such as polarization. They show that existing witnesses miss genuine hybrid number-polarization entanglement in macroscopic Bell states. The work derives an operational sufficient witness and outlines how it could be implemented experimentally.
main_result: The paper presents an operational witness for genuine hybrid number-polarization entanglement.
application_angle: Such certification can strengthen photonic quantum information protocols that exploit multiple optical degrees of freedom at once.

title: Toward Scalable Heterogeneous Quantum Networks: Microwave-Optical Transduction Across Platforms
abstract: The development of scalable quantum networks requires coherent interfaces capable of converting microwave photons used in superconducting quantum processors into optical photons suitable for long-distance fiber transmission. This review surveys recent progress in microwave-to-optical quantum transduction across optomechanical, electro-optic, and magneto-optic platforms, with emphasis on conversion efficiency, bandwidth, added noise, and operating temperature. In addition to standard metrics, we propose the internal efficiency eta_in and the magnon decay rate kappa_m/2pi as normalized parameters that enable fairer comparison across heterogeneous implementations. Optomechanical systems achieve internal phonon-to-photon efficiencies of 93% with sub-quantum added noise of 0.25 quanta at millikelvin temperatures. Electro-optic devices based on LiNbO3 and AlN have advanced from room-temperature efficiencies below 1% to millikelvin systems with internal efficiencies approaching 99.5%, added noise as low as 0.16 quanta at 60 mK, and bandwidths extending to several tens of megahertz. Magneto-optic (optomagnonic) platforms exhibit the lowest efficiencies (typically $10^{-10}$ to $10^{-8})$, but offer intrinsic non-reciprocity and broadband magnonic operation, with emerging approaches based on topological heterostructures and magnon squeezing predicting enhancements up to $10^{-4}$. Optomechanical systems appear promising for high-fidelity quantum state transfer, electro-optic transducers for high-bandwidth coherent links, and magneto-optic devices for non-reciprocal network components. We discuss the fundamental trade-off between efficiency and added noise across all three platforms, and argue that heterogeneous microwave-optical transduction is emerging as a key enabling technology for distributed quantum computing and large-scale quantum networks.
one_liner: A review compares microwave-optical transduction across optomechanical, electro-optic, and magneto-optic platforms for quantum networks.
why_it_matters: It benchmarks the interfaces needed to connect superconducting processors to optical fiber links for distributed quantum computing.
detailed_summary: This review surveys coherent microwave-to-optical conversion using optomechanical, electro-optic, and magneto-optic devices, emphasizing efficiency, bandwidth, added noise, and temperature. It proposes normalized comparison metrics including internal efficiency and magnon decay rate. The review highlights optomechanical demonstrations with 93% internal phonon-to-photon efficiency and sub-quantum noise, electro-optic devices approaching 99.5% internal efficiency with low noise and MHz-scale bandwidth, and magneto-optic systems that trade low efficiency for non-reciprocal functionality.
main_result: The paper synthesizes platform metrics and trade-offs for transducers linking microwave quantum processors to optical networks.
application_angle: It clarifies which transduction technologies are best suited for high-fidelity state transfer, high-bandwidth links, and non-reciprocal network components.

title: Quantum criticality and factorization in a constrained Rydberg spin chain
abstract: We investigate the zero-temperature phase diagram of a one-dimensional constrained quantum spin chain realized in coherently driven Rydberg-atom arrays with competing local Rabi driving and dipole-dipole exchange interactions. Projecting onto the blockade-constrained Hilbert space yields an effective model in which local and nonlocal quantum fluctuations compete on equal footing. Combining exact diagonalization, the density-matrix renormalization group, and variational uniform matrix-product-state calculations, we establish a complete phase diagram comprising a Luttinger liquid, an antiferromagnetic ordered phase, and a polarized paramagnet. We identify two distinct mechanisms for the destruction of antiferromagnetic order: a conventional Ising transition at strong driving and a continuous quantum melting into the Luttinger liquid at weak driving, characterized using entanglement-based diagnostics and finite-entanglement scaling. In addition, we uncover an exact ground-state factorization line embedded within the ordered phase, providing an analytically tractable zero-entanglement reference point for experiments with programmable Rydberg quantum simulators.
one_liner: A constrained Rydberg spin-chain study maps critical phases and finds an exact factorization line for quantum simulators.
why_it_matters: It gives programmable Rydberg arrays concrete critical regimes and a zero-entanglement benchmark for hardware validation.
detailed_summary: The work studies a blockade-constrained one-dimensional Rydberg spin chain with competing local Rabi driving and dipole-dipole exchange. Using exact diagonalization, DMRG, and variational uniform matrix-product states, it maps a phase diagram containing a Luttinger liquid, antiferromagnetic order, and a polarized paramagnet. It identifies both Ising and continuous melting routes out of antiferromagnetic order and uncovers an exact zero-entanglement factorization line inside the ordered phase.
main_result: The study establishes the zero-temperature phase diagram and an exact factorized ground-state line in a constrained Rydberg model.
application_angle: The results provide experimentally accessible calibration targets and critical phenomena for analog Rydberg quantum simulators.

title: Impact of Stoichiometry of MoSi Thin Films for Enhanced Sensitivity of Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors
abstract: We report on the impact of the stoichiometry of superconducting MoSi thin films on the performance of superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs). Specifically, we investigate the relation between the film parameters critical temperature Tc , sheet resistance Rs and superconductor thickness d and observe a universal scaling behavior. To benchmark the performance of SNSPDs fabricated from films having different stoichiometry, we measure the bias dependent count rate curves, while the detector is illuminated with wavelengths between 780 nm and 1550 nm. The detector performance as a function photon energy for different nanowire widths reveals a linear relation between the detection current and the photon energy. Furthermore, we determine the interfacial thermal boundary conductance $β$ between the superconducting thin film and the substrate, by measuring the return current of the SNSPD and find an increase of $β$ with increasing Mo concentration. The highest sensitivity amongst all compared devices is achieved for Mo$_{0.53}$Si$_{0.47}$, with low Tc (4.1 K) and high Rs (397$Ω$/sq) at a film thickness of 5.4 nm.
one_liner: MoSi stoichiometry is tuned to improve SNSPD sensitivity from 780 to 1550 nm, with Mo0.53Si0.47 giving the best device response.
why_it_matters: Material composition directly links film Tc, sheet resistance, thermal coupling, and photon sensitivity, giving a practical knob for better near-IR SNSPDs.
detailed_summary: The study compares superconducting MoSi thin films with different stoichiometries and connects Tc, sheet resistance, and thickness through a universal scaling behavior. SNSPDs made from these films are benchmarked using bias-dependent count-rate curves under 780-1550 nm illumination. The authors find a linear relation between detection current and photon energy, and extract interfacial thermal boundary conductance from return-current measurements. Mo0.53Si0.47 at 5.4 nm thickness gives the highest sensitivity, combining low Tc and high sheet resistance.
main_result: Mo0.53Si0.47 SNSPDs achieved the highest measured sensitivity across the compared stoichiometries.
application_angle: The results guide fabrication of more sensitive telecom and near-IR single-photon detectors for quantum optics and communication systems.

title: Qiskit QuantumKatas: Adapting Microsoft's Quantum Computing exercises for LLM evaluation
abstract: We adapt Microsoft's QuantumKatas -- a well-established quantum computing curriculum -- from Q# to Qiskit, the most widely-adopted quantum computing framework, and package it with an evaluation framework for systematic LLM assessment. The resulting benchmark comprises 350 tasks across 26 categories, spanning fundamental gates through advanced algorithms (Grover's, Simon's, Deutsch-Jozsa), error correction, key distribution, and quantum games. Each task includes a natural language prompt, canonical solution, and deterministic test verification via classical circuit simulation. By building on the QuantumKatas' proven pedagogical design rather than creating tasks from scratch, we inherit a principled difficulty progression and comprehensive concept coverage while contributing the framework adaptation, evaluation infrastructure, and empirical analysis. We evaluate 16 LLMs across 7 prompting configurations -- a total of 39,200 model runs -- to demonstrate the benchmark's utility. Three key findings emerge: (1) the benchmark effectively differentiates model capabilities, with best-configuration pass rates ranging from 32.3% to 83.1% and a 26.1 pp average gap between frontier and open-source models; (2) models perform well at implementing known algorithms (SimonsAlgorithm 82.1%, BasicGates 81.6%) but struggle with problem encoding (SolveSATWithGrover 34.4%, DistinguishUnitaries 40.0%); and (3) chain-of-thought prompting shows a modestly bimodal effect -- it is the best strategy for three models (two of them explicitly reasoning-tuned per vendor documentation) but degrades performance for the rest, leaving it mid-pack in aggregate (56.3% mean) behind few-shot-5 (57.8%). We release the benchmark, evaluation framework, and baseline results to support research on LLM capabilities in quantum computing.
one_liner: QuantumKatas are ported from Q# to Qiskit as a 350-task benchmark for evaluating LLM quantum-programming ability.
why_it_matters: Reliable quantum-software benchmarks help assess whether LLMs can assist real quantum hardware workflows beyond generic coding tasks.
detailed_summary: The work adapts Microsoft's QuantumKatas curriculum into Qiskit and packages it with deterministic classical simulation tests. The benchmark covers 350 tasks across 26 categories, from gates and algorithms to error correction, QKD, and quantum games. Evaluation of 16 LLMs over 39,200 runs shows broad performance separation, with strong results on known algorithms and weaker results on problem encoding. Prompting strategy effects are model-dependent, with chain-of-thought helping only some models.
main_result: The authors release a tested 350-task Qiskit benchmark and baseline results across 16 LLMs.
application_angle: This supports practical assessment of AI assistants used for quantum circuit development, education, and hardware-facing software workflows.

title: Time Crystals on Quantum Devices
abstract: Time crystals are nonequilibrium phases of matter characterized by the emergence of temporal ordering, in which an interacting many-body system develops robust structure in its time evolution that is not trivially dictated by the external driving or environment. While related phenomena have long been studied in classical nonlinear systems, their realization in entangled quantum matter represents a distinct frontier. The theoretical understanding of discrete time crystals has substantially advanced, yet recent experiments using modern quantum devices and quantum processors reveal regimes beyond established paradigms. These developments call for an extended classification of time-crystalline phases according to both their stabilization mechanisms and their physical character, including discrete and continuous, closed and open, critical, topological, quasiperiodic, and controlled realizations. We review recent implementations of time crystals on quantum platforms and propose such a classification framework, identifying promising directions for the discovery of novel time-crystalline phases of matter.
one_liner: A review classifies time-crystal realizations on modern quantum devices across stabilization mechanisms and physical regimes.
why_it_matters: Time crystals are becoming experimentally accessible on quantum processors, making a device-oriented taxonomy useful for hardware exploration and sensing concepts.
detailed_summary: The paper reviews recent implementations of time-crystalline behavior on quantum devices and processors. It argues that experiments now probe regimes beyond the standard discrete time-crystal paradigm. The authors propose a broader classification covering discrete and continuous, closed and open, critical, topological, quasiperiodic, and controlled realizations. The focus is on how stabilization mechanisms and observable physical character organize future experiments.
main_result: The main contribution is an extended classification framework for time-crystalline phases realized on quantum platforms.
application_angle: A clearer taxonomy can help design quantum hardware experiments that use robust temporal order for metrology, simulation, or controlled nonequilibrium phases.

title: From Provable to Practical: A Problem-Driven Survey of Classical and Machine-Learning Defenses for DV/CV Quantum Key Distribution
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) promises information-theoretic security, yet practical deployments in discrete-variable (DV) and continuous-variable (CV) settings remain exposed to device imperfections, channel manipulation, finite-key effects, and vulnerabilities in machine-learning (ML) components used for adaptation and monitoring. This survey adopts a problem-driven perspective based on nine practical problem classes (P1-P9) spanning device, channel, protocol, ML, and network layers. For each class, we compare classical defenses with ML-enabled solutions including anomaly detection, parameter prediction, noise estimation, adversarial purification, and resource allocation. Reported results include DBSCAN-based CV attack detection at P=99.7%, R=99.8%, F1=0.998, adversarial robustness recovery up to 79.5%, channel-amplification detection at 100%/91.26% under low/high-noise conditions, and LightGBM-based noise prediction reducing evaluation time by up to 98.8%. The survey further proposes a benchmarking framework combining datasets, stress protocols, and unified evaluation metrics including SKR impact, maximum distance, latency, and robustness. Finally, we provide defense-in-depth deployment guidelines and outline future research directions for secure and practical QKD systems.
one_liner: A problem-driven survey maps classical and ML defenses for practical DV and CV QKD across device, channel, protocol, and network layers.
why_it_matters: Operational QKD security depends on defending imperfect devices and adaptive systems, not only proving idealized protocol security.
detailed_summary: The survey organizes practical QKD vulnerabilities into nine problem classes spanning hardware, channels, protocols, ML components, and networks. It compares classical mitigations with ML-enabled approaches such as anomaly detection, noise prediction, adversarial purification, and resource allocation. Reported examples include high-precision CV attack detection, channel-amplification detection, and large reductions in evaluation time using LightGBM noise prediction. The paper also proposes benchmark metrics covering secret-key-rate impact, range, latency, and robustness.
main_result: The paper provides a defense taxonomy, performance survey, and benchmarking framework for practical QKD deployments.
application_angle: It is directly relevant to building deployable QKD networks that remain secure under device imperfections, attacks, and adaptive control loops.

title: A multilevel tensor network compression technique for simulating Lindblad dynamics in superconducting circuits
abstract: Designing superconducting quantum hardware requires simulation tools that can account for various deviations from ideal scenarios. This, in turn, requires approaches that automatically detect certain structures and leverage them to make the computation affordable. Here, we develop a tensor network based technique to simulate the Lindblad dynamics of a few interacting bosonic modes with a focus on superconducting quantum circuits. The technique detects and takes advantage of two very common situations: (i) the density matrix being pure or not far from pure and (ii) the entanglement between different modes being moderate (typically qubit-like). However, (iii) the occupation of the modes can be arbitrarily high (making naïve truncations inefficient). To leverage these features, we use three different nested levels of tensor network compression: (i) we work with a global purification of the density matrix, (ii) we compress the connection between different modes to account for the moderate entanglement and (iii) we use a quantics representation of the Fock occupation number. We showcase the technique for the simulation of large cat qubits as well as for the ionization of transmon qubits, demonstrating orders-of-magnitude speed-up with respect to brute force approaches. In the latter example, it brings the simulation, previously reported on a large supercomputing infrastructure, to laptop level. The favorable scaling with system size should bring genuine computer assisted design of these systems within scope.
one_liner: A nested tensor-network method accelerates Lindblad simulations of superconducting circuits with high bosonic occupations.
why_it_matters: Efficient open-system simulation can move superconducting-circuit design problems from supercomputers toward routine computer-aided hardware design.
detailed_summary: The authors introduce a multilevel tensor-network compression scheme for open quantum dynamics in interacting bosonic modes. The method combines global purification, compression between modes for moderate entanglement, and a quantics representation of Fock occupation numbers. This handles states that may have very high mode occupation while still exploiting near-purity and qubit-like intermode entanglement. Demonstrations include large cat qubits and transmon ionization, with orders-of-magnitude speedups over brute-force methods.
main_result: The technique enables laptop-scale simulations for cases previously requiring much heavier computation, including transmon ionization dynamics.
application_angle: It can make iterative design and validation of superconducting qubits, cat qubits, and leakage mechanisms much more practical.

title: Displaced Gaussian Boson Sampling for enhanced max-clique search
abstract: Gaussian Boson Sampling (GBS) is capable of solving certain classes of graph problems owing to the samples produced by such a device having a connection to the hafnian matrix function. In particular, a GBS device has been shown to provide an enhancement in the search of cliques -- or complete subgraphs -- in undirected weighted graphs over classical algorithms. A graph can be mapped to a GBS experiment by configuring the squeezing parameters of the input states and programming the linear optical network. In practice, limited squeezing and photon loss degrade the performance of the GBS device for max-clique search. In comparison, coherent states -- often considered a classical resource due to their Poissonian statistics -- can be readily prepared across many modes using an attenuated laser. In this paper, we report an enhancement of the success rate of GBS in finding maximum weighted cliques by adding displacements under lossy conditions or when a limited amount of squeezing is available. Moreover, we report that this enhancement can be scaled up to large graphs with limited resource overheads.
one_liner: Displacements are added to Gaussian boson sampling to improve maximum weighted clique search under loss or limited squeezing.
why_it_matters: The proposal targets a practical bottleneck in photonic optimization hardware: maintaining useful graph-search performance with realistic optical resources.
detailed_summary: The paper studies Gaussian boson sampling for maximum weighted clique search when realistic constraints reduce performance. It shows that adding coherent displacements can improve success rates under photon loss or limited squeezing. Because coherent states are easy to prepare across many modes, the added resource overhead is comparatively modest. The authors report that the enhancement can scale to large graphs with limited extra resources.
main_result: Displaced GBS improves maximum weighted clique success rates in lossy and low-squeezing regimes.
application_angle: This could make photonic sampling hardware more useful for near-term graph optimization tasks under experimentally realistic constraints.

title: A cavity-less architecture for high-power integrated frequency combs
abstract: Photonic chip-based frequency combs have emerged as a transformative platform, enabling compact, scalable, and high-performance multiwavelength sources with far-reaching impact across science and technology. Most commonly, these sources leverage the cavity enhancement of the nonlinearities to produce a spectrum of equidistant frequency lines via cascaded four-wave mixing in high-quality microresonators pumped with a continuous wave tone. While the presence of the resonator inherently enables low-power threshold operation, it also brings intrinsic limitations in efficiency, tunability, and power per line. Here, we propose and demonstrate a cavity-less approach for the generation of optical frequency combs on-chip, which relies on non-degenerate cascaded four wave mixing in dispersion engineered integrated photonic waveguides. The results presented here enable previously inaccessible regimes of pump-to-comb conversion efficiency, wide-range continuous line-spacing tunability, and power per line for coherent comb states. This work opens new research opportunities in nonlinear integrated photonics and pathways toward high-capacity optical interconnects, scalable photonic AI accelerators, and other power-constrained integrated systems.
one_liner: Demonstrates a cavity-less on-chip frequency comb using cascaded four-wave mixing in engineered waveguides.
why_it_matters: Removing the resonator trades low threshold for higher efficiency, tunable spacing, and larger per-line power in integrated comb sources.
detailed_summary: The work proposes and demonstrates an integrated frequency-comb architecture that avoids high-Q microresonator cavities. It uses non-degenerate cascaded four-wave mixing in dispersion-engineered photonic waveguides to generate coherent comb states. The reported regime emphasizes high pump-to-comb conversion efficiency, continuously tunable line spacing, and high optical power per comb line.
main_result: A cavity-less waveguide comb generates coherent, high-power lines with wide continuous spacing tunability and strong pump-to-comb conversion.
application_angle: The architecture targets power-constrained multiwavelength systems such as optical interconnects, photonic accelerators, and compact nonlinear photonic sources.

title: Observing conformal Floquet dynamics on a digital quantum processor
abstract: Quantum simulations are traditionally confined to exploring dynamics starting from unentangled or low-entanglement states due to severe bottlenecks in protocol design, hardware performance, and classical verification. Here, we report the first experimental observation of non-equilibrium dynamics initiated directly from a many-body critical state. Using a fully-connected trapped-ion processor, we prepare the critical ground state of a transverse-field Ising model via a hardware-tailored, logarithmic-depth quantum circuit based on multi-scale entanglement renormalization. Following this initialization, we apply a deep Floquet drive that maintains emergent conformal symmetry, enabling us to benchmark the lattice dynamics against analytical results from continuum theory. In the resulting conformal heating phase, we extract a central charge consistent with the Ising universality class ($c=1/2$) from the universal decay of the Loschmidt echo and observe spatial energy localization predicted by field theory. Conversely, the non-heating phase exhibits global finite-time revivals. This work establishes a scalable and versatile framework for exploring critical quantum dynamics.
one_liner: Observes conformal Floquet dynamics initialized from a critical many-body state on a trapped-ion quantum processor.
why_it_matters: It shows digital quantum hardware can prepare critical states and probe field-theory dynamics beyond low-entanglement initial conditions.
detailed_summary: The experiment uses a fully connected trapped-ion processor to prepare the critical ground state of a transverse-field Ising model with a hardware-tailored logarithmic-depth circuit inspired by multi-scale entanglement renormalization. A deep Floquet drive then preserves emergent conformal symmetry, allowing comparison with continuum analytical predictions. The team observes conformal heating, extracts a central charge consistent with the Ising value c=1/2 from Loschmidt echo decay, and sees predicted spatial energy localization; the non-heating regime shows finite-time revivals.
main_result: The processor initializes a many-body critical state and experimentally resolves conformal heating, energy localization, and non-heating revivals under Floquet driving.
application_angle: This provides a scalable experimental route for studying critical nonequilibrium quantum dynamics and benchmarking quantum simulators against field theory.

title: Radiative Response of Atomic Systems Illuminated with Approximate Spherical Vector Waves
abstract: The natural electromagnetic modes spontaneously emitted by an atom in free space are spherical vector waves (SVWs). Each SVW mode is uniquely linked to a specific dynamical--spherical--multipole--moment of the atomic system. In this work, we introduce a general formalism for evaluating spherical multipole transition rates under different boundary conditions, considering the superposition between a given SVW and the modes resulting from the boundary conditions. This formalism is applied to study the radiative properties of an atomic system trapped near the focus of a 4$π$ optical array. By appropriately selecting the external light field, the juxtaposed lenses of the optical array allow the atom to be illuminated with approximate spherical vector waves. Explicit expressions for the resulting multipole transition rates are presented as a function of the numerical aperture of the lenses. The feasibility of enhancing and inhibiting electric dipole-forbidden transitions using such an array, under current experimental capabilities, is briefly discussed.
one_liner: Develops a formalism for atomic multipole transition rates under approximate spherical vector wave illumination.
why_it_matters: Controlled spherical-vector-wave excitation could enhance or suppress dipole-forbidden transitions for atom-photon interfaces.
detailed_summary: The paper formulates transition-rate calculations for atomic spherical multipole moments when boundary conditions mix a target spherical vector wave with other electromagnetic modes. It applies the theory to an atom near the focus of a 4π optical array, where paired high-NA lenses approximate spherical vector wave illumination. The resulting expressions connect multipole transition rates to lens numerical aperture and assess feasible enhancement or inhibition of electric dipole-forbidden transitions.
platform_or_system: atom in a 4pi optical lens array
main_result: The formalism predicts how approximate spherical vector wave illumination can tune atomic multipole transition rates under realistic numerical apertures.
application_angle: Such control can improve engineered atom-light coupling for quantum networks, precision spectroscopy, and optical control of normally weak transitions.

title: Sympathetic Cooling in Trapped Ions with Spectral Selectivity via the Zeeman Shift
abstract: High-fidelity quantum logic operations in trapped ions often require the ions' collective motion to be cooled to near the ground state. Since cooling the ions' motion typically involves dissipative processes such as spontaneous photon scattering, sympathetic cooling is used on select coolant ions between gate sequences to cool the ion chain without affecting the data qubits. Common implementations for coolant ions include different atomic species, different isotopes of the same species or individually addressable ions. Each of these approaches have challenges associated with them, which include increased hardware complexity, reduced efficiency of radial mode cooling and re-ordering events which add additional experimental overhead. We demonstrate a sympathetic cooling scheme leveraging internal metastable atomic levels accessible via a narrow quadrupole transition, utilizing the natural Zeeman shift and individually addressed Raman transitions, to achieve isolation of the non-coolant or ``data ions" from coolant ions. We demonstrate modest decoherence of the data ions due to cooling, while preserving the coherence requirements for high-fidelity gate operations.
one_liner: Demonstrates sympathetic cooling of trapped ions using Zeeman-shift spectral selectivity within one ion species.
why_it_matters: The method reduces hardware complexity while preserving data-ion coherence during cooling between logic operations.
detailed_summary: The work introduces a trapped-ion sympathetic cooling scheme that separates coolant and data ions spectrally rather than by species, isotope, or strict spatial isolation. It uses internal metastable levels reached by a narrow quadrupole transition, the natural Zeeman shift, and individually addressed Raman transitions to isolate data ions from cooling light. The experiment reports modest data-ion decoherence while maintaining coherence compatible with high-fidelity gate requirements.
main_result: Zeeman-selective sympathetic cooling cools collective motion while limiting cooling-induced decoherence on data ions.
application_angle: This could simplify trapped-ion processor operation by avoiding mixed-species overhead, radial-mode cooling penalties, and ion-reordering events.

title: Leveraging Correlated Decoding for Bias-Tailored Compass Codes
abstract: Quantum error correction (QEC) is often implemented on hardware that experiences biased noise, where dephasing errors occur more frequently than other errors. This has motivated many recent efforts to develop bias-tailored QEC codes, such as the Clifford-deformed compass codes: a family of codes that achieve high thresholds under biased dephasing noise. We perform circuit-level simulations of the Clifford-deformed elongated compass codes under a biased noise model and evaluate code thresholds using standard minimum weight perfect matching (MWPM) and correlated MWPM. We find that correlated decoding enhances thresholds for all noise biases relative to standard MWPM under circuit-level noise. Our results demonstrate that correlated decoding leads to a higher relative gain in thresholds compared to standard MWPM when applied to codes with asymmetric stabilizers under biased noise.
one_liner: Shows correlated decoding improves circuit-level thresholds for bias-tailored Clifford-deformed compass codes.
why_it_matters: Better decoding for biased-noise codes can lower physical error-rate demands in hardware dominated by dephasing.
detailed_summary: The paper studies Clifford-deformed elongated compass codes under a biased circuit-level noise model, comparing standard minimum-weight perfect matching with correlated MWPM. Simulations show correlated decoding increases thresholds across all tested noise biases. The relative threshold gain is especially notable for asymmetric stabilizer codes tailored to biased dephasing noise.
main_result: Correlated MWPM decoding raises circuit-level thresholds for Clifford-deformed elongated compass codes under biased noise.
application_angle: The result informs decoder and code choices for superconducting, spin, or other qubit platforms where dephasing-biased noise is prevalent.

title: 18-dB on-chip vacuum squeezing in an adaptively poled lithium niobate waveguide
abstract: Quantum squeezed states of light can enhance measurement sensitivity beyond classical limits and enable quantum information processing, but scalable low-loss sources remain challenging. We demonstrate continuous-wave quantum squeezing on a chip, achieving 18 dB of squeezing and 20 dB of anti-squeezing at 1570 nm in a 1.6-cm traveling-wave adaptively poled thin-film lithium niobate waveguide. A distributed model independently determines facet losses, phase noise, and nonlinear interaction strength without prior assumptions, enabling rigorous inference of on-chip performance. We estimate a 95% confidence interval of [-18.96, -17.25] dB squeezing and [19.96, 21.35] dB anti-squeezing. These values represent the highest squeezing reported for any integrated photonic platform and the first assumption-free statistical validation of integrated squeezing performance. Our results establish thin-film lithium niobate as a high-performance, scalable platform for continuous-variable quantum sensing, communications, and photonic computing.
one_liner: Reports 18 dB continuous-wave on-chip vacuum squeezing in a thin-film lithium niobate waveguide.
why_it_matters: This sets a new integrated-photonics squeezing benchmark with statistical validation of loss, phase noise, and nonlinear strength.
detailed_summary: The experiment generates continuous-wave squeezed vacuum at 1570 nm in a 1.6 cm traveling-wave adaptively poled thin-film lithium niobate waveguide. It reports 18 dB squeezing and 20 dB anti-squeezing, with a distributed model that independently infers facet losses, phase noise, and nonlinear interaction strength. The stated 95% confidence interval is [-18.96, -17.25] dB for squeezing and [19.96, 21.35] dB for anti-squeezing, claimed as the highest squeezing for an integrated photonic platform.
platform_or_system: adaptively poled thin-film lithium niobate waveguide
main_result: The chip achieves statistically validated 18 dB-class on-chip continuous-wave vacuum squeezing at telecom wavelength.
application_angle: High integrated squeezing directly supports compact quantum sensors, continuous-variable quantum communication, and photonic computing systems.

title: Additive binding energies in asphalt on a quantum processor via quantum-selected configuration interaction (QSCI)
abstract: Quantum-centric supercomputing (in which a quantum processor samples the dominant electronic configurations and classical high-performance computing resources perform the diagonalisation) is emerging as a practical route to correlated electronic-structure calculations. We present QuantumPave, a hybrid quantum-classical workflow for computing additive binding energies in asphalt binder, a quantity central to the oxidative ageing of road infrastructure. Using a 24-atom pyridine-phenol hydrogen-bonded complex as a representative model, we couple machine-learning interatomic potentials (ORB v3) for geometry optimisation with quantum-selected configuration interaction (QSCI), also referred to as sample based quantum diagonalisation (SQD), in a (10e, 10o) active space run on the 54-qubit IQM Emerald processor. On hardware, SQD reproduces the active-space CASCI reference exactly, giving a binding energy of -3.52 kcal/mol (-0.153 eV); the device noise broadens the sampling to span the active space, so no zero-noise extrapolation is required. This active-space value captures the static correlation within the chosen orbitals and underbinds the full hydrogen bond: a counterpoise-corrected CCSD(T) benchmark gives -8.5 to -9.5 kcal/mol, while the calorimetric enthalpy of about -6.25 kcal/mol is consistent with this once zero-point, thermal, and solvent contributions are included. We show that chemically meaningful binding energies for an industrially relevant materials problem are attainable on current quantum hardware within a quantum-centric supercomputing workflow.
one_liner: QuantumPave computes asphalt-model binding energies on a 54-qubit processor using QSCI/SQD with classical HPC diagonalization.
why_it_matters: It shows a current quantum processor can contribute chemically meaningful energetics for an industrial materials problem, even with limited active spaces.
detailed_summary: The work applies a quantum-centric workflow to a pyridine-phenol hydrogen-bonded complex modeling asphalt binder chemistry. ORB v3 potentials optimize geometries, while a 54-qubit IQM Emerald processor samples configurations for QSCI/SQD in a (10e,10o) active space followed by classical diagonalization. Hardware sampling reproduces the active-space CASCI binding energy of -3.52 kcal/mol, while higher-level CCSD(T) benchmarks indicate missing dynamic correlation in the chosen active space.
main_result: The hardware SQD workflow exactly reproduced the active-space CASCI reference and yielded a -3.52 kcal/mol binding energy.
application_angle: The target is oxidative aging in road asphalt, making the demonstration relevant to materials durability and infrastructure chemistry.

title: A Note on Boosting Uncloneable Encryption in Microcrypt
abstract: In this note, we consider the setting of uncloneable encryption satisfying uncloneable indistinguishability, a form of symmetric key encryption that prevents the cloning of ciphertexts in a very strong sense. Our goal is to minimize the assumptions under which (many-time secure) uncloneable encryption is known to exist, assuming the existence of an information-theoretic "uncloneable bit", i.e. a one-time secure uncloneable encryption scheme for one-bit messages. We observe that if a t -> t' uncloneable bit exists, then the following implications hold. 1. If many-time secure symmetric key encryption exists, then many-time secure t -> t' uncloneable encryption for arbitrary-length messages exists. Since many-time secure uncloneable encryption implies many-time secure symmetric key encryption, this result is tight. 2. If pseudorandom unitaries exist, then many-time secure t -> t' uncloneable encryption for arbitrary-length messages with identical copy security exists. These results together show that many-time secure uncloneable encryption may follow from concrete assumptions in "microcrypt", the world of unstructured quantum cryptography that plausibly exists even if P = NP.
one_liner: This note shows how to boost one-time uncloneable bits into many-time uncloneable encryption under minimal cryptographic assumptions.
why_it_matters: It narrows the assumptions needed for quantum-secure ciphertexts that resist copying, a core primitive for future quantum communication security.
detailed_summary: The paper studies uncloneable indistinguishability for symmetric-key encryption, starting from an information-theoretic one-time uncloneable bit. It proves that many-time secure symmetric-key encryption plus a t-to-t' uncloneable bit gives many-time uncloneable encryption for arbitrary messages. With pseudorandom unitaries, the construction also achieves identical-copy security, situating the result in the low-assumption microcrypt regime.
main_result: A one-time uncloneable bit can be boosted to many-time arbitrary-length uncloneable encryption under tight minimal assumptions.
application_angle: The result informs practical quantum communication protocols where intercepted ciphertexts should not be copyable for later exploitation.

title: Three-fold coincidence by stimulated parametric down-conversion
abstract: Parametric down-conversion is a widely used source of nonclassical light in quantum optics and photonic quantum technologies. While stimulated parametric down-conversion with strong classical seeds is well studied, the regime in which stimulation occurs at the single-photon level has hitherto remained largely unexplored experimentally. Here, we study continuous-wave, low-gain down-conversion seeded by a weak coherent field with an average photon number well below one per coherence time. By measuring third-order temporal correlations, we observe a clear enhancement that cannot be accounted for by spontaneous processes or accidental coincidences alone, and is consistent with stimulation involving the seed and the generated photon pair. These results provide time-domain evidence of seed-induced three-photon correlations and suggest new ways to engineer and probe multi-photon states for quantum imaging, sensing, and information processing.
one_liner: Weak single-photon-level seeding enhances three-fold coincidences in continuous-wave low-gain parametric down-conversion.
why_it_matters: It experimentally probes a little-tested regime between spontaneous and classically stimulated PDC, opening routes to engineered multiphoton photonic states.
detailed_summary: The experiment studies continuous-wave, low-gain parametric down-conversion seeded by a weak coherent field with less than one photon per coherence time. Third-order temporal correlation measurements show an enhancement beyond spontaneous emission and accidental coincidences. The signal is consistent with stimulation involving the seed and the generated photon pair, giving time-domain evidence for seed-induced three-photon correlations.
main_result: The authors observed a clear seed-induced enhancement in third-order coincidences at the single-photon stimulation level.
application_angle: Controllable three-photon correlations could support quantum imaging, sensing, and photonic information-processing sources.

title: Photon-Conditioned Squeezed States for Directional Displacement Response in Continuous-Variable Photonics
abstract: Squeezed Fock states, photon-subtracted squeezed states, and optical cat states are established non-Gaussian resources in continuous-variable quantum optics. Here we compare these known state families from a task-oriented perspective: matched mean photon number, scalar Wigner negativity, and directional displacement-fidelity response. Starting from squeezed vacuum, single-photon subtraction prepares a state proportional to \(S(r,θ)\ket{1}\), while two-photon subtraction prepares an even-parity squeezed Fock superposition rather than a pure \(S(r,θ)\ket{2}\). We benchmark photon-conditioned squeezed states against Fock and coherent-cat references using the integrated Wigner negativity \(δ\), the energy-normalized metric \(δ/\langle n\rangle\), and fidelity-threshold displacement radii \(R_F(φ)\). Cat benchmarks remain strong scalar Wigner-negativity resources, whereas photon-conditioned squeezed states provide an origin-centered alternative with tunable anisotropic displacement response. In particular, the two-photon-subtracted squeezed state shows favorable displacement-fidelity radii over selected quadrature directions at matched \(\langle n\rangle\). These results identify a regime relevant to homodyne-aligned continuous-variable control and anisotropic displacement-noise mitigation, with directional sensing as a natural dual application.
one_liner: Photon-conditioned squeezed states are benchmarked as tunable non-Gaussian resources with directional displacement response.
why_it_matters: The analysis links state preparation choices to anisotropic displacement sensitivity, useful for CV photonic control and sensing under directional noise.
detailed_summary: The paper compares squeezed Fock states, photon-subtracted squeezed states, and cat states at matched mean photon number using Wigner negativity and displacement-fidelity radii. It clarifies that single-photon subtraction from squeezed vacuum gives a squeezed one-photon state, while two-photon subtraction creates an even-parity squeezed Fock superposition. The two-photon-subtracted state shows favorable direction-dependent fidelity radii along selected quadratures.
main_result: Two-photon-subtracted squeezed states offer tunable anisotropic displacement response at matched energy.
application_angle: The framework points to CV photonic states optimized for homodyne-aligned control, directional sensing, and displacement-noise mitigation.

title: Exciton-Tunable Phase Control and Superstrong Coupling Through Multimode Polariton Engineering in Planar Waveguides
abstract: Strong light-matter coupling in optical waveguides provides a versatile platform for engineering hybrid polaritonic modes and their dispersion. Here we investigate multimode exciton-photon coupling in visible semiconductor waveguides supporting several transverse electric modes. Using rigorous coupled-wave analysis combined with a coupled-oscillator model, we show that the photonic band structure can be engineered across a range of regimes, from conventional multimode strong coupling to the superstrong coupling regime, where the Rabi splitting becomes comparable to the spacing between adjacent photonic modes. In the latter regime, hybridization between orthogonal electromagnetic modes is enabled by restricting the active material to a subregion of the mode volume where the photonic modes exhibit strong mutual overlap. This breaking of the orthogonality leads to polaritonic branches whose composition can be tuned among several photonic modes and the exciton. We demonstrate that small shifts of the exciton resonance produce pronounced changes in the propagation constants of different polariton branches, enabling exciton-controlled phase modulation through modal interference and, in the superstrong coupling regime, direct modal switching across a continuous S-shaped dispersion. The resulting figures of merit predict $π$ phase shifts for exciton energy shifts of only a few meV over propagation lengths of tens of micrometers, while larger shifts are still required for mode switching. These results establish multimode waveguide polaritons as a versatile platform spanning multiple coupling regimes, for compact phase and intensity control in integrated photonic architectures.
one_liner: Multimode semiconductor waveguide polaritons enable exciton-tuned phase control and superstrong coupling across hybrid branches.
why_it_matters: It proposes compact integrated-photonic phase and intensity control using small exciton-energy shifts over tens of micrometers.
detailed_summary: The study models visible semiconductor planar waveguides with multiple transverse electric modes using rigorous coupled-wave analysis and coupled oscillators. By localizing active material where modes overlap, the design breaks modal orthogonality and mixes several photonic modes with an exciton. In the superstrong regime, small exciton shifts strongly alter propagation constants, producing predicted pi phase shifts over tens of micrometers and enabling possible modal switching for larger shifts.
main_result: The model predicts exciton-controlled polariton phase shifts of pi for only a few meV exciton tuning over compact propagation lengths.
application_angle: This could enable dense integrated optical modulators and switches based on polaritonic dispersion engineering.

title: Memory-assisted squeezed light velocimetry under realistic loss and incoherent noise
abstract: We propose a velocity sensor based on a two-memory Mach--Zehnder interferometer fed by a coherent probe and squeezed vacuum, read out by balanced homodyne detection. One memory is taken as a stationary reference, while the second memory moves during storage, so that its velocity is mapped onto a differential interferometric phase at readout. The two memories are otherwise assumed identical and are described by a Gaussian write--store--read lifetime together with the associated unconditional noise floor. Using the classical Fisher information, we derive the velocity sensitivity, the transmission threshold required for a target quantum gain, and the optimum storage time. The squeezed scheme improves on equal-resource coherent homodyne within an operating window set mainly by total transmission and phase stability. For representative near-term parameters, unconditional memory noise floors up to about $10^{-1}$ photons per trial do not by themselves remove the advantage; after optimization the improvement remains at the few-percent level and is limited chiefly by loss.
one_liner: A two-memory squeezed-light Mach-Zehnder scheme maps memory motion into phase for loss-aware quantum velocimetry.
why_it_matters: It quantifies when squeezed probes still improve velocity sensing despite memory loss, phase instability, and incoherent noise floors.
detailed_summary: The proposal uses a Mach-Zehnder interferometer with coherent light and squeezed vacuum, storing one arm in a stationary memory and the other in a moving memory. Motion during storage becomes a differential phase read out by balanced homodyne detection. Classical Fisher information yields the velocity sensitivity, transmission threshold, and optimal storage time, showing few-percent optimized gains for near-term parameters mainly limited by loss.
main_result: The squeezed-memory velocimeter can outperform equal-resource coherent homodyne within a transmission- and phase-stability-limited operating window.
application_angle: The scheme is relevant to precision velocity sensing with quantum memories in realistic lossy photonic systems.

title: Lattice-Trapped Atom Interferometry with a Bose-Einstein condensate: Observation and Control of Interactions
abstract: Precision interferometry with atomic wavepackets confined in a one-dimensional optical lattice is an emergent paradigm in quantum sensing of forces and fields, with applications in gravimetry, accelerometry, geophysics, and fundamental physics tests. We report on the realization of a lattice-trapped interferometer where the two arms are sourced from a weakly-interacting ytterbium Bose-Einstein condensate, coherently split and trapped by pulsed optical standing waves before recombination. We directly observe atomic interactions through contrast changes and phase shifts of the interferometer. By changing either the atom number or the sample volume to vary the density, we demonstrate control over interactions and optimize interferometer performance. Our observations are effectively captured by a mean-field theoretical model of the system. This work experimentally probes the boundary where improved performance from source brightening through higher phase space density transitions into a regime beyond single-atom physics in lattice-trapped atom interferometry, and opens a door to incorporating many-body effects for metrological advances in such platforms.
one_liner: Ytterbium BEC wavepackets are split, lattice-trapped, and recombined to study interaction-driven atom-interferometer phase and contrast shifts.
why_it_matters: It maps the regime where brighter quantum-degenerate atom sources improve sensing before many-body interactions limit or reshape interferometer performance.
detailed_summary: The experiment realizes a one-dimensional lattice-trapped atom interferometer using a weakly interacting ytterbium Bose-Einstein condensate as the source. Pulsed optical standing waves coherently split, hold, and recombine the wavepackets, while density is tuned through atom number or sample volume. The authors directly observe interaction-induced contrast changes and phase shifts and model them with mean-field theory.
platform_or_system: ytterbium BEC in a 1D optical lattice
main_result: The work demonstrates controllable mean-field interaction effects in a lattice-trapped BEC atom interferometer and uses them to optimize performance.
application_angle: This is directly relevant to compact gravimeters, accelerometers, geophysics sensors, and precision tests that need bright atom sources without uncontrolled interaction shifts.

title: Automatic Attenuation Control for Mitigating Photon-Counting Saturation in SPAD-based Optical Wireless Communications
abstract: Single-photon avalanche diodes (SPADs) have emerged as a promising candidate for optical wireless communication (OWC) owing to their ultra-high sensitivity and singlephoton detection capability. However, under strong background radiation or high signal power, SPAD-based receivers suffer from photon-counting saturation, which severely degrades communication performance. To address this challenge, this paper introduces an automatic attenuation control (AAC) technique that dynamically optimizes the incident optical intensity to mitigate saturation effects. We develop a comprehensive analytical model for the SPAD-based OWC system, incorporating the influence of dead time and the lack of photon-number resolution. Based on this model, a convex optimization-based AAC algorithm is proposed to maximize the achievable rate in real time. Furthermore, a low-complexity AAC algorithm is devised using a closed-form trigger probability criterion, reducing computational complexity by two orders of magnitude. Numerical results demonstrate that the proposed AAC technique significantly improves both the achievable rate and symbol error rate across a wide range of background conditions, providing an efficient solution to enhance the dynamic range of photon-counting receivers.
one_liner: Adaptive optical attenuation is proposed to keep SPAD photon-counting receivers out of saturation in optical wireless links.
why_it_matters: It addresses a practical dynamic-range bottleneck for ultra-sensitive photon-counting optical receivers under strong signal or background light.
detailed_summary: The paper models SPAD-based optical wireless communication including dead time and lack of photon-number resolution, then optimizes incident intensity with automatic attenuation control. A convex real-time algorithm maximizes achievable rate, while a closed-form trigger-probability rule cuts complexity by about two orders of magnitude. Simulations show improved rate and symbol error rate over broad background-radiation conditions.
main_result: The proposed AAC schemes dynamically mitigate photon-counting saturation and improve communication performance across varying optical power and background levels.
application_angle: The approach could make photon-counting OWC receivers more robust for outdoor, mobile, and high-dynamic-range free-space optical links.

title: Coherent Dark State Formation of a Lead-Vacancy Spin Qubit in Diamond
abstract: A lead-vacancy (PbV) center in diamond exhibits coherent emission above the liquid helium temperature, making it highly attractive for quantum network applications. Here, we report the magneto-optical and spin properties of PbV centers in diamond. We record a spin lifetime of 12 ms at 7.5 K under large off-axis magnetic field. Furthermore, we observe formation of the coherent dark state by coherent population trapping and estimate a spin dephasing time of 354 ns at 6.5 K. This work demonstrates the outstanding thermal robustness of the PbV spin compared to other group-IV centers above 4 K.
one_liner: PbV centers in diamond show coherent dark-state formation and millisecond spin lifetime above liquid-helium temperature.
why_it_matters: Thermally robust group-IV color centers are strong candidates for scalable spin-photon interfaces in quantum networks.
detailed_summary: The authors characterize magneto-optical and spin properties of lead-vacancy centers in diamond under cryogenic but above-4 K conditions. They measure a 12 ms spin lifetime at 7.5 K with a large off-axis magnetic field and observe coherent population trapping. From dark-state spectroscopy they estimate a 354 ns spin dephasing time at 6.5 K.
main_result: The paper demonstrates coherent dark-state formation in a PbV spin qubit and reports robust spin coherence metrics above liquid-helium temperature.
application_angle: PbV centers could support less demanding cryogenic quantum-network nodes with optically addressable spins and coherent photon interfaces.

title: Mid-Infrared Single-Photon Compressive Spectroscopy
abstract: Sensitive mid-infrared (MIR) spectroscopy plays an indispensable role in various photon-starved conditions. However, the detection sensitivity of conventional MIR spectrometers is severely limited by excessive noises of the involved infrared sensors, especially for multi-pixel arrays in parallel spectral acquisition. Here, we devise and implement an ultra-sensitive MIR single-pixel spectrometer, which relies on high-fidelity spectral upconversion and wavelength-encoding compressive measurement. Specifically, a MIR nanophotonic supercontinuum from 3.1 to 3.9 $μ$m is nonlinearly converted to the near-infrared band via synchronous chirped-pulse pumping, which facilitates both the precise spectral mapping and sensitive upconversion detection. The upconverted signal is then spatially dispersed onto a programmable digital micromirror device, before being registered by a single-element silicon detector. Consequently, the spectral information can be deciphered from the correlation between encoded patterns and recorded measurements, which results in a spectral resolution of 0.5 cm$^{-1}$ under an illumination flux down to 0.01 photons/nm/pulse. Moreover, we demonstrate faithful reconstructions at sub-Nyquist sampling rates by using the compressive sensing algorithm, which leads to a 95\% reduction in data acquisition time. The presented single-pixel computational spectrometer features wavelength multiplexing, high throughput, and efficient sampling, which thus paves a new way for sensitive and fast spectroscopic analysis at the single-photon level.
one_liner: A MIR single-pixel spectrometer combines upconversion and compressive encoding for single-photon-level spectroscopy.
why_it_matters: It bypasses noisy MIR detector arrays while retaining high sensitivity, high throughput, and fast spectral acquisition.
detailed_summary: The system converts a 3.1-3.9 um MIR nanophotonic supercontinuum into the near-infrared using synchronous chirped-pulse pumping, enabling sensitive silicon detection. The upconverted spectrum is dispersed onto a programmable digital micromirror device for wavelength-encoded single-pixel measurements. It achieves 0.5 cm^-1 resolution at flux down to 0.01 photons/nm/pulse and reconstructs spectra with 95% less acquisition time via sub-Nyquist compressive sensing.
main_result: The work demonstrates high-resolution MIR spectra at single-photon-level flux with compressive acquisition that greatly reduces measurement time.
application_angle: This can improve trace-gas, chemical, biomedical, and remote spectroscopy in photon-starved environments where MIR arrays are too noisy.

title: Noise adaptive two-way secure deterministic quantum key distribution
abstract: We introduce noise-adaptive quantum key distribution (QKD) protocols, in which the honest parties optimize the encoding (state preparation) and decoding (measurement basis) operations according to the noise models affecting the honest subsystems induced by an eavesdropper. This extends conventional QKD schemes that employ fixed encoding and decoding strategies independent of the noise characteristics of the communication channel. We investigate three representative protocols: entanglement-based secure dense coding (SDC), the entanglement-free Lucamarini and Mancini (LM05), and a two-way prepare-and-measure Bennett Brassard (BB84) protocols. Using entropic uncertainty relations, we derive the corresponding secret key rates for both adaptive and conventional non-adaptive scenarios under collective attacks. For independent but identical noise acting on the forward and backward transmission channels, as well as for correlated and non-Markovian environments, we identify classes of channels for which adaptive schemes yield enhanced secret key rates for the considered protocols. In contrast, we also determine Pauli channels, including depolarizing and bit flip channels, for which adaptive strategies provide no benefit. We further show that these optimal sets are generally non-unique and can differ substantially from the unitaries that maximize dense-coding capacity in the absence of security constraints. Our results establish noise-adaptive encoding and decoding as a powerful framework for improving secure communication over realistic noisy quantum channels.
one_liner: Noise-adaptive encoding and measurement choices are analyzed for two-way deterministic QKD protocols over realistic noisy channels.
why_it_matters: It shows when channel-aware operations can raise secure key rates beyond fixed-basis QKD designs, and when they cannot.
detailed_summary: The paper develops noise-adaptive versions of entanglement-based secure dense coding, LM05, and two-way prepare-and-measure BB84 protocols. Using entropic uncertainty relations, it derives secret key rates under collective attacks for adaptive and non-adaptive choices. Adaptive strategies help for selected independent, correlated, and non-Markovian channels, but not for Pauli cases such as depolarizing and bit-flip channels.
main_result: The main result is a security-rate framework identifying channel classes where adaptive encoding and decoding improve two-way QKD key rates.
application_angle: The results guide practical QKD systems that can calibrate channel noise and tune transmitter and receiver operations for better secure throughput.

title: Squeezed-slit Bohr-Einstein Interferometer
abstract: The Einstein-Bohr recoiling-slit gedankenexperiment, a cornerstone of quantum complementarity, has long been constrained by the zero-point fluctuations of the atomic slit -- the spatial Standard Quantum Limit (SQL). Here we transcend this fundamental boundary through active quantum state engineering of a single-atom slit. By implementing a non-adiabatic quench-evolve-quench protocol, we prepare the atomic motion in a squeezed state, dynamically redistributing phase-space uncertainty to suppress which-path information and restore high-visibility interference beyond the static vacuum limit. We report an intrinsic visibility of $0.938_{-0.008}^{+0.004}$, violating the SQL ($0.819$) by over 10 standard deviations, corresponding to $7.6(2)$ dB of effective squeezing. Our work reveals Kerr-induced non-Gaussian dynamics and reinterprets the traditional interferometer as a powerful tool for continuous-variable Wigner tomography, bridging the gap between quantum foundations and advanced metrology.
one_liner: A squeezed atomic slit beats the spatial SQL in a Bohr-Einstein interferometer, restoring high-visibility interference.
why_it_matters: It turns a foundational complementarity test into an engineered continuous-variable platform relevant to precision metrology.
detailed_summary: The experiment engineers the motional state of a single-atom slit through a non-adiabatic quench-evolve-quench protocol. By squeezing the slit motion, it redistributes phase-space uncertainty, suppresses which-path information, and raises interference visibility beyond the static vacuum SQL. The reported intrinsic visibility is 0.938 with an SQL of 0.819, corresponding to 7.6(2) dB effective squeezing and evidence of Kerr-induced non-Gaussian dynamics.
main_result: The work demonstrates interference visibility beyond the spatial standard quantum limit by actively squeezing the recoiling atomic slit.
application_angle: The technique points toward metrological interferometers that use engineered motional quantum states to control backaction and enhance visibility.

title: Cavity-Induced Suppression of Entanglement and Enhancement of Quantum Discord
abstract: We study correlations between two Unruh-DeWitt detectors coupled to a scalar field in a cylindrical cavity. Boundary conditions strongly modify the detector-correlation dynamics relative to free space. The entanglement negativity is suppressed in the cavity and vanishes for smaller separation as compared to the free space. Increasing the cavity radius does not recover the free-space behavior of the negativity. In contrast, mutual information and quantum discord remain nonzero over much larger separations. While the mutual information decays monotonically with separation, the quantum discord is enhanced near the cavity boundary. Our results demonstrate that geometric confinement can selectively suppress distillable entanglement while preserving and even enhancing more general non-classical correlations, providing a controlled setting to probe the hierarchy of correlations in quantum field theory.
one_liner: A cylindrical cavity reshapes field-mediated detector correlations, suppressing entanglement while preserving discord over longer ranges.
why_it_matters: It clarifies how engineered boundaries can tune different quantum correlations, relevant to cavity QED tests and communication protocols beyond distillable entanglement.
detailed_summary: The paper studies two Unruh-DeWitt detectors coupled to a scalar field inside a cylindrical cavity and compares their correlations with free-space behavior. Cavity boundary conditions suppress entanglement negativity, causing it to vanish at shorter detector separations, and increasing radius does not simply recover the free-space limit. Mutual information and quantum discord remain finite over larger distances, with discord enhanced near the cavity boundary.
platform_or_system: cylindrical cavity field with two detectors
main_result: Geometric confinement selectively suppresses distillable entanglement while preserving and locally enhancing broader non-classical correlations.
application_angle: The results suggest cavity geometry as a control knob for quantum-correlation resources in field-mediated communication and cavity-QED experiments.

title: Environment-Enhanced Single-Photon Absorption in a Nano-Ring of Dipole-Coupled Quantum Emitters
abstract: Decoherence is mostly detrimental in quantum information and quantum optics applications. However, the interplay between environment-induced incoherent dynamics and unitary evolution can give rise to novel quantum many-body phenomena that can be harnessed as a useful resource. As is well known, in dense subwavelength atomic arrays only a single collective eigenmode in the single-excitation manifold couples strongly to free-space radiation, exhibiting superradiant spontaneous emission. Most of the remaining eigenstates form a manifold of weakly radiative modes, giving rise to long-lived subradiant excitations. Here we demonstrate that populating these subradiant modes via additional decoherence mechanisms, such as dephasing or coupling to phonons, can significantly enhance single-photon absorption in a nanoring of quantum emitters. Such nanoring geometry is particularly appealing due to its unique optical properties and its resemblance to natural light-harvesting complexes, which serve as efficient antennas in photosynthesis. Our findings may shed light on fundamental aspects of energy absorption in nature; despite the much greater complexity of biological systems, they may nonetheless operate according to similar underlying optical principles.
one_liner: Decoherence-assisted population of subradiant modes boosts single-photon absorption in a dipole-coupled quantum-emitter nanoring.
why_it_matters: It turns environmental noise into an absorption resource for nanoscale quantum antennas, with links to artificial light harvesting and photonic interfaces.
detailed_summary: The work analyzes a dense subwavelength ring of dipole-coupled quantum emitters, where one bright superradiant state couples strongly to radiation while many subradiant modes are weakly radiative. Added incoherent processes such as dephasing or phonon coupling redistribute population into these long-lived modes. This environment-assisted pathway can substantially enhance single-photon absorption compared with purely coherent dynamics.
main_result: Controlled decoherence can enhance single-photon absorption by populating subradiant collective modes in a nanoring antenna.
application_angle: The mechanism could inform efficient nanoscale photon absorbers, quantum optical antennas, and bio-inspired light-harvesting devices.

title: Quantum Spin Squeezing Enhanced by Critical Exceptional Points
abstract: Critical exceptional points (CEPs) are nonequilibrium critical points in open many-body systems at which multiple collective excitation modes coalesce. CEPs are known to amplify classical fluctuations, but their effect on genuinely \textit{quantum} fluctuations remains unclear. Here, we show that dissipative collective-spin systems hosting CEPs exhibit parametrically enhanced steady-state \textit{quantum} spin squeezing. Close to the CEP, the optimally squeezed variance scales as $|Z|$, whereas the anti-squeezed variance diverges as $|Z|^{-1}$, with $Z$ the dimensionless order parameter. Importantly, the anti-squeezed fluctuation direction asymptotically aligns with the coalescing eigenvector of the stability matrix, reflecting the defective nature of the CEP dynamics. These scalings are robust against dephasing channels generated by spin components orthogonal to the coalesced critical collective mode. Our results identify CEPs as a route to engineering steady-state anisotropic quantum fluctuations and correlations in driven-dissipative platforms.
one_liner: Critical exceptional points in driven-dissipative collective spins generate parametrically enhanced steady-state spin squeezing.
why_it_matters: It identifies non-Hermitian criticality as a route to engineered quantum fluctuations for metrology and correlated photonic or atomic platforms.
detailed_summary: The paper shows that dissipative collective-spin systems near critical exceptional points exhibit strong anisotropic quantum fluctuations. The optimally squeezed variance scales as |Z|, while the anti-squeezed variance diverges as |Z|^-1, where Z is the dimensionless order parameter. The anti-squeezed direction aligns with the coalescing eigenvector of the defective stability matrix, and the scaling is robust to certain orthogonal dephasing channels.
main_result: Critical exceptional points provide a parametric enhancement of steady-state quantum spin squeezing in open many-body dynamics.
application_angle: Enhanced squeezing can improve precision sensing and guide fluctuation engineering in atomic, spin, and photonic non-Hermitian platforms.

title: Learning Logical Operations for Arbitrary Quantum Error Correction Codes
abstract: Logical operations are essential for quantum computation within quantum error-correcting codes. However, discovering their physical realizations is challenging, especially for non-additive codes that lack a stabilizer description. We present a general learning-based framework that, given only an encoding circuit, constructs physical implementations of logical operations while enforcing structural properties such as transversality or shallow depth. Our approach is validated by rediscovering known logical operations of standard stabilizer codes. We then extend it to a co-design procedure, dubbed variational early fault-tolerant quantum computing (VarEFTQC), which tailors non-additive encodings to a given noise model and enforces desired logical gate sets, such as transversal IQP-type families or low-depth universal sets. A software library implements the complete learning pipeline, including loss-function variants, ansatz families, and optimization routines. Together, these results position VarEFTQC as a practical tool for discovering hardware-adapted logical gadgets for early fault-tolerant quantum computing.
one_liner: A learning framework constructs physical logical gates for arbitrary QEC codes using only an encoding circuit.
why_it_matters: It offers a practical path to hardware-adapted logical operations, including for non-additive codes that lack stabilizer structure.
detailed_summary: The authors present a learning-based method that takes an encoding circuit and searches for physical implementations of logical operations with constraints such as transversality or shallow depth. The framework rediscovers known logical operations for standard stabilizer codes, then extends to VarEFTQC, a co-design procedure for tailoring non-additive encodings to hardware noise and target logical gate sets. A software library implements ansatz families, loss variants, and optimization routines for the pipeline.
platform_or_system: encoded quantum circuits and QEC codes
main_result: The framework learns constrained physical realizations of logical gates for arbitrary quantum error-correcting codes from encoding circuits alone.
application_angle: It can help design early fault-tolerant logical gadgets matched to real device noise, connectivity, and gate constraints.

title: Universal zero-crosstalk photonic integration via slab-engineered mode hybridization
abstract: Photonic integrated circuits have emerged as a scalable platform for optical computing, communication, and quantum technologies, where high-fidelity optical processing is essential. However, as photonic systems scale in complexity, inter-channel crosstalk accumulates across cascaded components, fundamentally degrading signal fidelity, limiting system-level performance, and constraining integration density. Existing crosstalk-suppression strategies rely on specialized nanostructures or platform-specific designs, hindering their adoption in standard foundry processes and across diverse material systems. Here we establish a universal and foundry-compatible route to eliminating crosstalk based on slab-engineered mode hybridization in standard rib waveguides. By tailoring the slab thickness, mode hybridization induces anisotropic modal perturbations that enable complete cancellation of coupling between adjacent waveguides. We experimentally demonstrate zero-crosstalk across diverse material platforms, including silicon-on-insulator, silicon nitride, thin-film lithium niobate, and germanium-on-insulator, spanning wavelengths from the visible to the mid-infrared. Our approach provides a manufacturable route toward scalable, high-fidelity, and high-density photonic integration, overcoming the long-standing trade-off between signal fidelity and integration density in large-scale photonic systems.
one_liner: Slab-engineered mode hybridization cancels adjacent-waveguide coupling across multiple foundry-compatible photonic platforms.
why_it_matters: Zero-crosstalk routing directly addresses density and fidelity limits in scalable photonic computing, communications, and quantum PICs.
detailed_summary: The paper introduces a foundry-compatible crosstalk-cancellation method for standard rib waveguides based on tuning slab thickness to induce anisotropic mode perturbations. This engineered hybridization enables complete cancellation of coupling between adjacent waveguides without relying on platform-specific nanostructures. Experiments demonstrate zero crosstalk in SOI, silicon nitride, thin-film lithium niobate, and germanium-on-insulator across visible to mid-infrared wavelengths.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrate universal zero-crosstalk waveguide integration across several major photonic material platforms.
